title: The Quiet Underground
summary: How the soil microbiome works — the decomposers, nitrogen-fixers, and extremophiles that build the ground beneath every garden. Bridges microbiology to ecology and the broader cross-portfolio biome cast.

questions.prompt: The soil microbiome is the community of microbes that live in…
choices: Dirt, compost, and the spaces between grains of soil
Only on top of the ground, never beneath
questions.explanation: A single teaspoon of healthy soil contains more microbes than there are people on Earth. They live in the tiny spaces between grains, sharing nutrients with roots.

questions.prompt: Decomposer microbes like Loam fungi help the soil by…
choices: Breaking down dead leaves and old wood into nutrients plants can use
questions.explanation: Decomposers are the recyclers of the ground. Every fallen leaf becomes part of next year's soil — that's their work.

questions.prompt: Rhizobium bacteria live near plant roots and help by…
choices: Pulling nitrogen from the air and turning it into a form plants can use
questions.explanation: Plants can't use nitrogen from the air directly. Rhizobium does that work — a partnership that lets clover, beans, and peas grow strong.

questions.prompt: Thermophile microbes are special because they…
choices: Thrive in very hot environments where most life cannot
Cannot survive outside the human body
questions.explanation: Thermophiles live in hot springs, deep-sea vents, and inside compost heaps. They show that life finds a way in places most creatures couldn't survive.

questions.prompt: Compost-rich soil helps a garden grow because it…
choices: Feeds a diverse community of microbes that release nutrients to roots
Removes all microbes from the soil
questions.explanation: Diverse compost feeds diverse microbes. Diverse microbes feed roots in slow, steady ways — which is why a compost-rich garden often grows quieter and stronger.

questions.prompt: Soil microbes and plant roots usually work together in a way called…
questions.explanation: Many soil microbes share nutrients with roots in exchange for sugars from the plant. It's one of nature's oldest partnerships — quiet, underground, and everywhere.
